title: Informal Conjecture (f(n) = 2n−2)
statement_text: We suspect that the above construction reflects the truth and that $f(n) = 2n - 2$ for all $n \geq 2$.
context_text: The authors give a construction (attributed to Segal-Halevi [6]) showing $f(n) \geq 2n-2$ for all $n \geq 2$, where $f(n)$ is the maximum number of cuts needed for disproportionate division with $n$ agents. Theorem 1.1 establishes $f(n) \leq 3n-4$, leaving a gap. The authors believe the lower bound is tight and that the truth is $f(n) = 2n-2$.

title: Conjecture 3.1
statement_text: For any $n \geq 2$ non-atomic probability measures $\mu_1, \mu_2, \ldots, \mu_n$ on the unit circle $S^1$ and non-negative reals $\alpha_1, \alpha_2, \ldots, \alpha_n$ summing to $1$, there exists a partition of the set $[n] = P \cup Q$ into two nonempty sets and a partition of the circle $S^1 = X \cup X^c$ into two intervals such that $$\min_{i \in P} \mu_i(X) = \sum_{j \in P} \alpha_j \quad \text{and} \quad \min_{i \in Q} \mu_i(X^c) = \sum_{j \in Q} \alpha_j.$$
context_text: The authors introduce this conjecture as a topological measure-partitioning statement whose truth would imply, by a simple inductive argument, that $f(n) = 2n-2$ for all $n \geq 2$, which would be optimal for disproportionate division. The $n=2$ case is known and is essentially the Stromquist–Woodall theorem, but the conjecture is open already for $n=3$.
notes: PDF source — the display-math formula in the original is garbled in extraction (sum symbols rendered as 'X', superscript 'c' split off); statement reconstructed from context.
